extracted_characteristics.dt_services:
The **Digital Twin (DT)** system provides **real-time monitoring, simulation, and lifecycle-encompassing optimization** services tailored for **Facility Managers, architects, engineers, and construction professionals**. Key service capabilities include:
  - **Monitoring**: Real-time synchronization of physical asset states via **Eclipse Hono and Ditto**, enabling live data integration for operational oversight.
  - **Simulation**: Performance and scenario-based simulations derived from **BIM models**, supporting **design decision coordination** and **clash detection** (inherited from BIM collaborative platforms).
  - **Control**: Model-driven automation for **operational control DTs**, enabling reproducible deployments and reducing manual intervention.
  - **Visualization**: Interactive 3D/4D representations of **semantic building models**, facilitating **collaborative decision-making** and **life-cycle-aware analytics**.
  The system targets **scalable DT engineering** in AECO, bridging **design-time models** (e.g., BIM) with **runtime operations** for domain-wide adoption.
extracted_characteristics.tooling_and_enablers: {'software_platforms': [{'name': 'Eclipse Hono', 'role': 'Real-time data integration platform enabling live synchronization between physical assets and digital twins', 'functionality': 'Handles device connectivity, message routing, and data ingestion for IoT-enabled assets'}, {'name': 'Eclipse Ditto', 'role': 'Digital twin management framework', 'functionality': 'Provides twin data management, twin lifecycle management, and twin state synchronization'}], 'development_frameworks': [{'name': 'Model-driven automation framework', 'role': 'Core enabler for reproducible DT deployment', 'functionality': 'Supports automated deployment processes, reducing manual intervention and enabling scalable DT engineering'}, {'name': 'Modular architecture with extension points', 'role': 'Service-oriented development platform', 'functionality': 'Allows scalable, extensible DT development tailored to AECO requirements'}], 'technologies': [{'name': 'Design Science Research (DSR) methodology', 'role': 'Research framework', 'functionality': 'Guides development of artifacts for model-driven deployment of operational control DTs'}, {'name': 'BIM (Building Information Modeling)', 'role': 'Foundational modeling paradigm', 'functionality': 'Provides semantically rich, object-based 3D models for digital twin representation'}, {'name': 'IFC/COBie standards', 'role': 'Data exchange standards', 'functionality': 'Ensures interoperability between BIM and DT systems'}], 'development_tools': [{'name': 'Prototyping and experimentation tools', 'role': 'Validation framework', 'functionality': 'Supports iterative development and testing of DT artifacts'}]}
extracted_characteristics.twinning_time_scale: The Digital Twin system supports **real-time data exchange capabilities** to ensure **continuous synchronization** between physical building components and their digital counterparts, addressing the dynamic operational needs of the AECO (Architecture, Engineering, Construction, and Operations) domain. The framework emphasizes **runtime configuration management**, enabling adjustments without full redeployment, which implies adaptable synchronization frequencies aligned with operational demands. However, the documents do not specify exact temporal granularities (e.g., millisecond, second, or sub-second intervals) or latency requirements beyond the general requirement for **real-time synchronization**. The system transitions from static BIM representations to dynamic operational monitoring, necessitating **low-latency bidirectional data flows** for interactive visualization and control, though precise update frequencies remain unspecified.
extracted_characteristics.multiplicities: The Digital Twin system adopts a **modular architecture with standardized extension points**, facilitating the deployment of **multiple Digital Twin instances** tailored to specific roles and responsibilities. The framework defines three primary roles: **Building Control Engineers** (responsible for device modeling and initial deployment via BIM tools), **Facility Managers** (interacting with deployed DTs for real-time monitoring and control), and **DT Engineers** (extending framework capabilities through service integration). The system operates as a **centralized hub**—the DT Framework—orchestrating bidirectional data exchange and enabling a **holistic life cycle management** approach. While the architecture supports **scalability from individual components to entire infrastructure systems**, the documents do not explicitly detail decentralized or hybrid models, loose coupling, or trade-offs between centralized/decentralized coordination mechanisms. The focus remains on a **unified, role-specific deployment model** with clear separation of responsibilities.
extracted_characteristics.dt_constellation:
The Digital Twin system follows a **centralized hub-and-spoke architecture**, where the **DT Framework** acts as the **orchestration core**, coordinating roles (Building Control Engineers, Facility Managers, DT Engineers) and components (BIM models, operational data, custom services). Key orchestration patterns include:
   - **Bidirectional data exchange** between physical assets and digital models, enabled by **runtime configuration management** for adaptability.
   - **Modular service integration** via standardized extension points, allowing **plug-and-play customization** without full redeployment.
   - **Resource management** is implied through **low-overhead updates** and **scalability** from component-level to infrastructure-wide systems.
   The framework leverages **coordinated roles** (as per Figure 1) to manage the DT lifecycle, with the hub facilitating **holistic life cycle management** by bridging BIM’s static representations and dynamic operational needs. No explicit mention of **microservices**, **event-driven architectures**, or **containerization** is provided, but the modularity suggests compatibility with such patterns for future scalability.
extracted_characteristics.horizontal_integration:
The Digital Twin framework integrates with **external systems** via standardized protocols and tools, including:
  - **Eclipse Hono** and **Ditto** for **real-time data exchange**, enabling seamless ingestion and processing of IoT/building sensor data.
  - **Interoperability standards** (implicitly referenced) to ensure compatibility with heterogeneous systems, though specific protocols (e.g., OPC UA, MQTT, REST) are not named.
  - **BIM tools** (e.g., Revit, ArchiCAD) for initial device modeling and deployment, acting as upstream data sources.
  The framework supports **enterprise system integration** through its modular architecture, allowing dependencies on external databases, CMMS (Computerized Maintenance Management Systems), or other AECO (Architecture, Engineering, Construction, Operations) domain tools. However, explicit integration patterns with **other Digital Twins** or **cross-organizational systems** are not documented. Non-functional requirements emphasize **scalability** and **maintainability**, suggesting horizontal integration is designed for **scalable, low-overhead updates** across infrastructure systems.
extracted_characteristics.dt_models_and_data:
The Digital Twin system employs a **model-driven deployment framework** that integrates **Building Information Modeling (BIM)** as the foundational data source, augmented to address its static limitations. The framework supports **geometric, behavioral, and analytical models** through enriched BIM representations, enabling Building Control Engineers to define **sensors, actuators, and controllers** directly within the BIM environment. This creates a **unified semantic foundation** for subsequent transformations via **Model-Driven Engineering (MDE) principles**, where structured representations are extracted from BIM models to generate **deployable Digital Twin artifacts** with minimal manual intervention.

Data components include:
- **Model types**: Geometric (BIM-based spatial representations), behavioral (control logic and device configurations), and analytical (simulation models for performance prediction).
- **Data sources**: Primarily BIM models enriched with **Building Control System (BCS) components**, supplemented by real-time data feeds from physical sensors and actuators.
- **Data management approaches**: **Automated extraction** of structured representations via MDE, ensuring traceability from BIM to DT artifacts. The framework supports **real-time data exchange** between physical buildings and their digital counterparts, synchronized through standardized interfaces.
- **Model relationships**: Hierarchical and modular relationships between BIM-based geometric models, behavioral control logic, and analytical simulation models, enabling seamless integration across the Digital Twin constellation. The framework’s **modular architecture** includes standardized extension points for integrating custom services and analytics, facilitating dynamic evolution of the DT’s data components.

The system emphasizes **interactive visualization** for immersive monitoring and control, leveraging the enriched BIM models as the primary data backbone for all subsequent operations.
extracted_characteristics.physical_acting_components:
The Digital Twin system incorporates **physical actuators and control mechanisms** primarily configured and deployed by Building Control Engineers within the BIM environment. While specific actuator types (e.g., HVAC valves, motorized shades, or smart relays) are not detailed in the documents, the framework supports their integration through **BIM-enriched device modeling**, enabling direct definition of control logic and actuation parameters.

Key aspects include:
- **Control capabilities**: Actuators are integrated with **behavioral models** defined in the BIM environment, allowing for **remote control interfaces** and automated actuation based on real-time data or predefined logic.
- **Actuation ranges and limitations**: Parameters such as operational limits, response times, and energy consumption are implicitly modeled within the BIM framework but are not explicitly quantified in the provided documents.
- **Safety constraints**: While not detailed, the framework’s **model-driven deployment** ensures that actuation commands are validated against the **unified semantic foundation** of the DT, likely incorporating safety checks during runtime.
- **Addressing mechanisms**: Actuators are addressed via **standardized extension points** in the modular architecture, enabling seamless communication with the Digital Twin’s control logic and visualization interfaces. The system supports **real-time synchronization** between physical actuators and their digital representations.

The framework does not explicitly mention **specific protocols** (e.g., OPC UA, Modbus) or **physical constraints** (e.g., power limits, mechanical ranges) for actuators, but their integration is facilitated through the BIM-based modeling process.
extracted_characteristics.physical_sensing_components:
The Digital Twin system models **sensors** directly within the **BIM environment**, enabling Building Control Engineers to define their configurations as part of the **Building Control System (BCS)**. Sensors are integrated to support **real-time data exchange** between the physical infrastructure and the digital twin.

Key characteristics include:
- **Sensor Types**: Defined generically in BIM as **sensors** (e.g., environmental, occupancy, or structural sensors), though specific types (e.g., temperature, CO₂, vibration) are not enumerated.
- **Measurement Capabilities**: Not specified; capabilities are inferred from the **BIM-defined parameters** and **runtime data streams**.
- **Spatial Distribution**: Implicitly determined by BIM’s geometric models, where sensors are placed within the building’s spatial context.
- **Sampling Frequencies/Data Transmission Rates**: Not detailed; however, **real-time data exchange** suggests high-frequency synchronization (e.g., milliseconds to seconds) for critical applications.
- **Accuracy and Precision Specifications**: Not provided; reliance on **BIM-defined sensor configurations** and **post-occupancy data** for validation.
- **Data Collection Mechanisms**: Sensors feed data into the DT via **real-time data exchange protocols** (e.g., Eclipse Hono/Ditto), ensuring continuous synchronization with the physical system.
extracted_characteristics.fidelity_and_validity_considerations:
The framework addresses **model fidelity** and **validity** through a structured approach to bridge the **gap between static BIM representations** and the **dynamic nature of Digital Twins**. Key considerations include:

- **Model Fidelity Requirements**:
  - **Semantic Enrichment**: BIM models are augmented with **domain semantics** (e.g., sensors, actuators) to enable dynamic DT functionality.
  - **Automated Extraction**: MDE principles reduce **manual errors** in model transformation, ensuring consistency between BIM and DT artifacts.

- **Validation and Verification Methods**:
  - **Overall Digital Twin Entanglement (ODTE) Metric**: Used to **evaluate the entanglement** of the resulting DT, quantifying the alignment between physical and digital states.
  - **Real-Time Synchronization**: Continuous **data exchange** ensures temporal fidelity, though specific validation protocols are not detailed.

- **Uncertainty Sources and Quantification**:
  - Not explicitly quantified; however, **sensor inaccuracies** and **model approximation errors** are potential sources, implicitly addressed via **runtime data validation**.

- **Error Detection and Correction Mechanisms**:
  - Not detailed; however, the **modular architecture** allows for **custom error-handling services/plugins** to detect and correct discrepancies.

- **Quality Assurance Processes**:
  - Focused on **reducing manual errors** through automation and **ensuring semantic consistency** via BIM-defined parameters. No explicit standards (e.g., ISO/IEC 15926) are referenced.

extracted_characteristics.life_cycle_stages:
The document outlines a **model-driven Digital Twin (DT) deployment framework** that addresses the gap between **static BIM representations** and the **dynamic nature of DTs**, supporting the **complete life cycle** of DT deployment and operation. Key phases and characteristics include:

    1. **Development Stages**:
       - **Device Modeling**: Building Control Engineers define sensors, actuators, and controllers **directly within BIM models**, creating a **unified semantic foundation** for subsequent steps. This addresses BIM’s limitation in representing Building Control System (BCS) components.
       - **Model Enrichment**: BIM models are **enriched** with structured representations (via Model-Driven Engineering, MDE principles) to enable transformation into deployable DT artifacts.
       - **Custom Service Integration**: DT Engineers develop and integrate **custom services and plugins** to extend DT functionality, allowing evolution with changing operational requirements.

    2. **Representation Types**:
       - **Static Representations**: BIM models serve as the **initial static foundation** for DTs, with limitations in dynamic or operational data.
       - **Dynamic Representations**: The framework generates **structured, deployable DT artifacts** (e.g., digital models with real-time data synchronization) to mirror physical infrastructure.
       - **Interactive Visualizations**: Immersive interfaces for monitoring and control are provided for **Facility Managers**, bridging static BIM and dynamic DT operations.

    3. **Transition Management**:
       - **Automated Transformation**: MDE principles enable **minimal manual intervention** in transitioning from BIM models to deployable DT artifacts.
       - **Real-Time Synchronization**: Continuous data exchange ensures **alignment between physical buildings and their digital twins**, supporting seamless transitions across operational phases.

    4. **Lifecycle Governance**:
       - **Modular Architecture**: Standardized extension points facilitate **scalable governance** of DT evolution, accommodating new services and analytics.
       - **Stakeholder Roles**: Governance is distributed among **Building Control Engineers** (modeling/control logic), **Facility Managers** (monitoring/operation), and **DT Engineers** (customization/extension).
       - **Use Case Alignment**: Governance aligns with **practical insights** from research projects (e.g., TwinLight, BOREALIS), ensuring relevance to real-world deployment scenarios.
extracted_characteristics.twinning_process_and_dt_evolution:
The document describes a **model-driven engineering (MDE) approach** for DT development and evolution, emphasizing automation, modularity, and stakeholder collaboration:

    1. **Methodologies**:
       - **Model-Driven Engineering (MDE)**: Leverages MDE to extract structured representations from enriched BIM models, enabling **automated generation of deployable DT artifacts**.
       - **Modular Architecture**: The framework’s design supports **plug-and-play integration** of custom services and analytics, allowing DTs to adapt to evolving requirements.
       - **Life Cycle Coverage**: Spans from **initial device modeling** to **runtime operation**, ensuring comprehensive support for all phases of DT deployment.

    2. **Quality Assurance (QA) Practices**:
       - **Semantic Consistency**: Unified semantic foundation from BIM models ensures **consistent data representation** across transitions.
       - **Automated Validation**: MDE-driven transformations reduce manual errors, though explicit QA processes (e.g., testing frameworks) are not detailed in the document.

    3. **Testing and Validation Approaches**:
       - **Not explicitly described** in the document. However, the framework’s **real-time synchronization** and **interactive visualization** imply validation through operational monitoring and user feedback.

    4. **Version Control**:
       - **Not explicitly mentioned**. The document focuses on **automated generation** and **modular evolution** but does not detail versioning strategies for DT artifacts or models.

    5. **Evolution Management Strategies**:
       - **Custom Service Integration**: DT Engineers extend functionality via **custom plugins/services**, enabling incremental evolution.
       - **Standardized Extension Points**: Modular architecture allows **scalable updates** without disrupting core DT operations.
       - **Real-Time Adaptability**: Continuous synchronization ensures DTs reflect **operational changes** in real time, supporting dynamic evolution.

    4. **User Interaction Methods**:

       - **Role-Based Access**: Stakeholders interact via **tailored workflows** (e.g., engineers model devices, managers monitor operations).

    5. **Confidence Measures for Recommendations**:

extracted_characteristics.standardization:
The framework adheres to **AECO-specific systems and standards**, particularly **Building Information Modeling (BIM)**, but lacks explicit compliance with broader **industry-wide DT standardization frameworks**. Key observations include:

1. **Standards Compliance:**
   - **BIM Alignment:** The framework is designed to be **compatible with existing AECO (Architecture, Engineering, Construction, and Operations) systems and standards**, leveraging BIM as the foundational data model for device and control logic representation.
   - **Tool-Specific Standards:** Integration with **Eclipse Hono** and **Ditto** (likely IoT middleware platforms) implies adherence to **IoT communication protocols**, though specific standards (e.g., OPC UA, MQTT) are not named.

2. **Specifications Followed:**
   - **Semantic Interoperability:** The framework enforces **semantic consistency** between BIM-enriched models and DT artifacts, ensuring interoperability within the AECO domain. However, **formal ontologies** (e.g., OWL, RDF) or **semantic web standards** are not referenced.
   - **Modular Interfaces:** Standardized extension points align with **service-oriented architecture (SOA) principles**, though no specific **API standards** (e.g., REST, gRPC) or **data exchange formats** (e.g., JSON-LD, XML) are mandated.

3. **Certification and Compliance Frameworks:**
   - **No Explicit Certifications:** The document does not mention compliance with **industry certifications** (e.g., ISO/IEC 23027 for DTs) or **regulatory frameworks** (e.g., GDPR for data handling).
   - **Absence of Standardized Frameworks:** The framework explicitly acknowledges the **absence of standardized integration frameworks** for DTs, focusing instead on **practical, domain-specific solutions** within AECO.

4. **Interoperability Standards:**
   - **AECO-Centric Interoperability:** Compatibility is ensured through **BIM-based data exchange** and **modular service integration**, but **cross-domain interoperability** (e.g., with non-AECO systems) is not addressed.
   - **Lack of Formal Standards:** No mention of **OMG MDA**, **IFC (Industry Foundation Classes)**, or **OPC UA** for DT interoperability, despite their relevance in the broader DT ecosystem. Integration is ad-hoc, relying on **tool-specific compatibility** (e.g., Unreal Engine 5, Eclipse Hono).

block_1_docs_retrieved:
_•_ **R2:** An automated deployment process that reduces manual intervention and enables reproducible DT setups ( **C2** ).

_•_ **R3:** Real-time data integration using Eclipse Hono and
Ditto, enabling live synchronization between physical
assets and digital twins ( **C3** ).

_•_ **R4:** A modular architecture with defined extension points
to support scalable, service-oriented DT development in
AECO ( **C4** ).

Together, these contributions aim to operationalize scalable
and interoperable DTs in AECO contexts. By closing the gap
between design-time models and runtime operations, we offer
a sustainable path toward domain-wide DT adoption.
Our work is organized using Design Science Research
(DSR) [59] and provides artifacts to facilitate model-driven deployment of operational control DTs in the AECO domain. We
start with requirements elicitation (cf. Section III-A) and conclude with prototyping and experimentation (cf. Section V).



Our artifacts are developed in response to the following design
science problem [59]:


**Improve** _DT_ _deployment_ _in_ _BIM-based_ _infrastructure_
_projects_ (context)
**by designing** _a model-driven automation framework_ (artifact)
**that satisfies** _semantic integration, real-time connectivity, and_
_extensibility_ (requirement)
**in order to** _enable scalable DT engineering in the AECO_
_domain_ . (goal)


III. BIM- BASED D IGITAL T WINS

BIM is increasingly regarded as the principal modelling
paradigm in the AECO sector, providing a comprehensive
framework for generating, managing, and disseminating digital
building information throughout the entire building life cycle
with policy initiatives and regulatory mandates decisively
fostering and accelerating this development [60]. BIM enables
the creation of semantically rich, object-based 3D models that
incorporate geometric, spatial, and functional characteristics
of building components and their relationships. These models provide a collaborative platform where architects, engineers, and construction professionals can coordinate design
decisions, detect clashes, simulate performance, and generate
documentation [61].
While there are also DTs and industry-specific solutions
for the digital mapping of assets in other industries, such
as the manufacturing industry (e.g. capitalizing on Modelbased Systems Engineering; MBSE) [1], [62], BIM is becoming the standard solution in the construction industry [63],

block_1_judge.reasoning: The extraction accurately describes the system as a BIM-based infrastructure in the AECO sector. It correctly captures the semantic richness of BIM models, the operational domain of building lifecycle management, and characteristics like policy mandates and lifecycle-centric approaches found in Section III. However, the specific mention of 'IFC, COBie' standards is not supported by the text, which only refers generally to 'standards such as BIM'. Quotes: 'BIM enables the creation of semantically rich, object-based 3D models', 'architecture, engineering, construction and operations (AECO) domain', 'generating, managing, and disseminating digital building information throughout the entire building life cycle'.

The extraction provides a highly specific and detailed summary of the services, matching the text's description of 'real-time monitoring, simulation, and life cycle-encompassing optimization'. It correctly links monitoring to 'Eclipse Hono and Ditto' for 'real-time data exchange' and identifies target users like 'Facility Managers' who 'monitors system states'. The description of simulation, control, and visualization capabilities is strongly grounded in the abstract and introduction.

The extraction is highly detailed and well-supported. It correctly identifies Eclipse Hono and Ditto for real-time data exchange, the model-driven automation framework for scalable deployment, and the modular architecture with extension points. It also accurately lists Design Science Research (DSR) as the methodology. Similar to the other characteristics, it includes an unsupported mention of IFC/COBie, where the text only mentions 'standards such as BIM'. Quotes: 'integrates Eclipse Hono and Ditto for real-time data exchange', 'model-driven framework for scalable DT deployment', 'standardized extension points to enable modular, service-oriented DT platforms', 'Our work is organized using Design Science Research (DSR)'.

block_2_docs_retrieved:
Real-time data exchange capabilities ensure continuous synchronization between physical buildings and their digital counterparts, while interactive visualization provides immersive interfaces for monitoring and control. The framework’s modular
architecture, with standardized extension points, supports the
integration of custom services and analytics, allowing DTs
to evolve with changing operational requirements. Runtime



configuration management further enhances adaptability by
enabling adjustments without requiring full redeployment.
Complementing these functional requirements, our nonfunctional requirements ( **NF** _X_ ) address critical aspects of
interoperability across heterogeneous systems, scalability from
individual components to entire infrastructure systems, maintainability through low-overhead updates, and accessibility for
users with varying technical expertise.
This requirements-driven approach provides a systematic
foundation for implementing DTs that bridge the gap between
BIM’s static representations and the dynamic operational
needs of the AECO domain, addressing the challenges identified in Section II. At this, we want to emphasize that these
are not all requirements, but rather those pertinent to crafting a
technological spike solution tailored to the specific challenges
and innovations explored in this work (cf. Section V-C). The
discussion section will revisit these requirements to evaluate
their effectiveness and role in advancing the proposed solution.


_B. Solution Proposal_


Figure 1 (p. 6) presents the conceptual model of our
framework, illustrating how the requirements from Table III
are implemented through coordinated roles and system components. Figure 1 demonstrates the flow from enriched BIM
models to operational DTs, emphasizing the key roles and their
contributions to the DT life cycle.
_Building Control Engineers_, using BIM tools, perform
device modeling and initiate the deployment process. _Facility_
_Managers_ then interact with the deployed DT for real-time
monitoring and control. _DT Engineers_ extend the framework’s
capabilities through service integration. The DT Framework
acts as a central hub, facilitating bidirectional data exchange
and enabling a holistic approach to building life cycle management.


IV. M ODEL  - BASED D EPLOYMENT OF D IGITAL T WINS

block_2_judge.reasoning: The extraction accurately captures the system's emphasis on 'real-time data exchange' and 'continuous synchronization' as described in the source documents. TABLE I: Actor Roles in our Model-driven DT Deployment
Framework


**Role** **Description**


Building Control Engineer Models and configures devices, defines the control logic, and initiates
DT deployment from BIM models.
Facility Manager Monitors system states, interacts
with the twin, and operates the
physical infrastructure via the DT.
DT Engineer Develops and integrates custom services and plugins to extend DT
functionality.


Commensurate with the limitations of BIM as a seamless

enabler for DTs, we have developed a comprehensive set of
requirements for our model-driven deployment framework.
These requirements emerge from both theoretical considerations and practical insights gained through our research
projects, TwinLight and BOREALIS (cf. Section II).
To address the gap between BIM’s static representations and
the dynamic nature of DTs, our proposal must support the
complete life cycle of DT deployment and operation. Table II
outlines the use cases to be supported by our framework,
spanning from initial device modeling to the development
of custom DT services atop our proposal. These use cases
reflect the core functionality needed by different stakeholders
(cf. Table I) involved in the DT life cycle, including _Building_
_Control Engineers_ who model devices and implement control
logic, _Facility Managers_ who monitor and control building operations, and _DT Engineers_ who extend functionality through
custom services.

The functional requirements ( **F** _X_ ) detailed in Table III form
the conceptual foundation of our solution, beginning with
device modeling capabilities that address BIM’s fundamental
limitation in representing BCS components. This enables
building control engineers to define sensors, actuators, and
controllers directly within the BIM environment, creating a
unified semantic foundation for subsequent transformation
steps. The framework then leverages MDE principles to extract
structured representations from these enriched BIM models
and automatically generate deployable DT artifacts with minimal manual intervention.

Real-time data exchange capabilities ensure continuous synchronization between physical buildings and their digital counterparts, while interactive visualization provides immersive interfaces for monitoring and control. The framework’s modular
architecture, with standardized extension points, supports the
integration of custom services and analytics, allowing DTs
to evolve with changing operational requirements. Runtime

block_4_docs_retrieved:
Real-time data exchange capabilities ensure continuous synchronization between physical buildings and their digital counterparts, while interactive visualization provides immersive interfaces for monitoring and control. The framework’s modular
architecture, with standardized extension points, supports the
integration of custom services and analytics, allowing DTs
to evolve with changing operational requirements. Runtime



configuration management further enhances adaptability by
enabling adjustments without requiring full redeployment.
Complementing these functional requirements, our nonfunctional requirements ( **NF** _X_ ) address critical aspects of
interoperability across heterogeneous systems, scalability from
individual components to entire infrastructure systems, maintainability through low-overhead updates, and accessibility for
users with varying technical expertise.
This requirements-driven approach provides a systematic
foundation for implementing DTs that bridge the gap between
BIM’s static representations and the dynamic operational
needs of the AECO domain, addressing the challenges identified in Section II. At this, we want to emphasize that these
are not all requirements, but rather those pertinent to crafting a
technological spike solution tailored to the specific challenges
and innovations explored in this work (cf. Section V-C). The
discussion section will revisit these requirements to evaluate
their effectiveness and role in advancing the proposed solution.


_B. Solution Proposal_


Figure 1 (p. 6) presents the conceptual model of our
framework, illustrating how the requirements from Table III
are implemented through coordinated roles and system components. Figure 1 demonstrates the flow from enriched BIM
models to operational DTs, emphasizing the key roles and their
contributions to the DT life cycle.
_Building Control Engineers_, using BIM tools, perform
device modeling and initiate the deployment process. _Facility_
_Managers_ then interact with the deployed DT for real-time
monitoring and control. _DT Engineers_ extend the framework’s
capabilities through service integration. The DT Framework
acts as a central hub, facilitating bidirectional data exchange
and enabling a holistic approach to building life cycle management.


IV. M ODEL  - BASED D EPLOYMENT OF D IGITAL T WINS

block_6_docs_retrieved:
[11]. Most frameworks are not designed with AECO-specific
standards like BIM in mind, and those that can be adapted
— such as Bentley’s iTwin.js [32] — rely on proprietary
ecosystems or require substantial manual effort due to a lack
of automation.

Recently, Gerber et al. [33] presented an ad-hoc DT for
a Living Lab for a LEED Platinum academic building based
on BIM for monitoring, simulation, and performance analysis.
Their study emphasizes automated data acquisition, normalization, and 3D visualization, while highlighting challenges
in data integration and stakeholder coordination, insights that
directly inform model-driven approaches in DT engineering.
Their results reinforce earlier findings by Opoku et al. [34]
who also demonstrated the effective integration of DTs with
BIM and IoT platforms to optimize indoor environmental conditions in a university library setting. Both studies underscore
the significance of real-time data utilization and visualization
in enhancing facility management and highlight common challenges in data integration and stakeholder engagement in DT
implementations. In synopsis, AECO adoption is dominated by
commercial tools or custom-built solutions [2], [13]. Notably,
MDE is not present across the surveyed works, leaving a
critical gap in abstraction and reuse capabilities essential for
complex domains like AECO.
Model-driven engineering (MDE) offers a streamlined approach to handling the increasing complexity of systems by
elevating models to primary engineering artifacts [35], [36].
It enables abstraction, formalization, and automation across
the development life cycle, reducing cognitive overload and
improving consistency in large-scale systems [37]. MDE supports the definition of domain-specific modeling languages
and metamodels that unify engineering workflows, providing
a structured foundation for communication across disciplines
and life cycle phases [38], [39]. Importantly, MDE also
strengthens governance by encoding organizational policies
and architectural decisions into formal models, promoting
compliance, reuse, and quality assurance [40]. Recent advances continue to refine the integration of MDE into DT
systems [41]–[47]. As reported in Lehner et al.’s recent survey,
research primarily focuses on runtime adaptability, semantic
coherence, and tool interoperability [48]. Abbasi et al. [49]
examined the phenomenon of _semantic drift_, where the alignment between digital models and their physical counterparts
diverges over time. Their study stresses the necessity of
synchronization techniques to ensure the integrity of modelbased DTs. Kamburjan et al. [50] proposed a declarative life
cycle specification language, enabling dynamic evolution and
rule-based management of DT instances. Meanwhile, Oakes et
al. [51] advanced ontological frameworks to support service
